content.text: Any autoregressive model conditions each token on its full history. The Markov assumption is a simplification: the future is conditionally independent of the distant past given the recent k tokens. A kth-order Markov model for sequences writes:

attrs.latex: P(w_t \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{t-1}) = P(w_t \mid w_{t-k}, \ldots, w_{t-1})

content.text: This is the same approximation that underlies N-gram language models (where k = n−1), hidden Markov models (order 1 over the hidden states), and many time-series models. The assumption is never exactly true in natural language, but it becomes accurate enough to be practically useful when combined with enough data and appropriate smoothing.

Without any independence assumption, modelling P(wₜ | w₁:t₋₁) exactly requires a separate parameter for every possible history. For a vocabulary of size V and history length t−1, this is V^t configurations — exponential in the sequence length. Even for V=1000 and t=5, that is 10¹⁵ parameters. The kth-order Markov assumption reduces this to V^(k+1) — the number of possible k-grams followed by one more token.
A first-order Markov chain is the simplest model: the next state depends only on the current state. The full joint probability factorises as:

attrs.latex: P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_T) = P(w_1) \prod_{t=2}^{T} P(w_t \mid w_{t-1})

content.text: For a discrete state space of size N, the model is fully specified by an initial distribution π (N-vector) and a transition matrix A (N×N, row-stochastic). Given training data, both are estimated by counting:

import numpy as np
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

def fit_markov_chain(sequences, vocab):
    """
    sequences: list of lists of tokens
    vocab: list of all possible tokens
    Returns: pi (N,), A (N,N)
    """
    idx = {w: i for i, w in enumerate(vocab)}
    N = len(vocab)
    init_counts   = Counter()
    trans_counts  = defaultdict(Counter)

    for seq in sequences:
        if not seq:
            continue
        init_counts[seq[0]] += 1
        for a, b in zip(seq, seq[1:]):
            trans_counts[a][b] += 1

    # Initial distribution
    total = sum(init_counts.values())
    pi = np.array([init_counts.get(w, 0) / total for w in vocab])

    # Transition matrix (add-1 smoothed)
    A = np.zeros((N, N))
    for i, w in enumerate(vocab):
        row_total = sum(trans_counts[w].values()) + N   # Laplace k=1
        for j, v in enumerate(vocab):
            A[i, j] = (trans_counts[w].get(v, 0) + 1) / row_total

    return pi, A

seqs = [
    ["sunny", "sunny", "rainy", "rainy", "sunny"],
    ["rainy", "sunny", "sunny", "sunny"],
    ["sunny", "rainy", "rainy"],
]
vocab = ["sunny", "rainy"]
pi, A = fit_markov_chain(seqs, vocab)
print("pi:", pi.round(3))
print("A:", A.round(3))
A kth-order Markov model treats the last k tokens as a single compound state. For language modelling, k=2 (trigram) is common:

Increasing k captures longer-range dependencies but inflates the number of states (and thus parameters) as N^k. For most NLP use cases, k > 5 is impractical with discrete representations; neural models avoid this by parametrising the context with a continuous embedding rather than enumerating all possible histories explicitly.
The same principle applies to continuous-valued time series. An AR(p) (autoregressive of order p) model assumes the current value depends linearly on the previous p values plus Gaussian noise:

import numpy as np

def fit_ar(x, p):
    """Fit AR(p) via OLS. x: 1-D array of observations."""
    T = len(x)
    # Design matrix: rows are (x[t-1], x[t-2], ..., x[t-p])
    X = np.column_stack([x[p-i-1:T-i-1] for i in range(p)])
    y = x[p:]
    # OLS: φ = (XᵀX)⁻¹ Xᵀy
    phi, residuals, _, _ = np.linalg.lstsq(X, y, rcond=None)
    return phi

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
# Simulate AR(2): x_t = 0.6 x_{t-1} + 0.2 x_{t-2} + noise
phi_true = np.array([0.6, 0.2])
T = 500
x = np.zeros(T)
for t in range(2, T):
    x[t] = phi_true @ x[t-2:t][::-1] + rng.normal(0, 0.5)

phi_hat = fit_ar(x, p=2)
print(f"True:      φ = {phi_true}")
print(f"Estimated: φ = {phi_hat.round(3)}")
# True:      φ = [0.6 0.2]
# Estimated: φ = [0.597 0.196]
A first-order Markov chain over a finite state space converges to a unique stationary distribution μ when it is ergodic (irreducible and aperiodic). The stationary distribution satisfies:

This is the left eigenvector of A corresponding to eigenvalue 1. The mixing time — how quickly the chain forgets its starting state — is governed by the second-largest eigenvalue of A. Chains with eigenvalues close to 1 mix slowly; chains with eigenvalues close to 0 mix quickly.

# Stationary distribution of the weather chain
A_weather = np.array([[0.7, 0.3],
                      [0.4, 0.6]])
eigenvalues, eigvecs = np.linalg.eig(A_weather.T)
idx = np.argmin(np.abs(eigenvalues - 1.0))
mu = eigvecs[:, idx].real
mu /= mu.sum()
print(f"Stationary: Sunny={mu[0]:.3f}, Rainy={mu[1]:.3f}")
# Stationary: Sunny=0.571, Rainy=0.429

# Second eigenvalue (≈ mixing speed)
lambda2 = sorted(np.abs(eigenvalues))[-2]
print(f"Second eigenvalue: {lambda2:.3f}")   # ~0.3 → fast mixing

Long-range dependencies violate finite-order Markov assumptions. In natural language, pronoun coreference ("The scientist … she"), topic coherence across paragraphs, and syntactic agreement across clauses all require information from far beyond the immediate context window. Time series may exhibit seasonality or structural breaks that small-order AR models cannot capture. Neural autoregressive models — RNNs and Transformers — represent the entire history implicitly and do not hard-commit to a fixed Markov order, bypassing this limitation.

The Markov assumption is arguably the single most important simplification in statistical sequence modelling. Without it, tractable probabilistic models of language, weather, genetics, finance, and speech are impossible: the state space grows exponentially with the length of the history. With it, we get a family of models — n-gram language models, hidden Markov models, autoregressive time series models — that are exact (within the assumption's validity), efficient to train by counting or least-squares, and interpretable.

Even modern Transformer language models implicitly use a Markov assumption of a fixed context window (4k, 8k, 128k tokens). The window size is the order of the effective Markov approximation. Understanding the Markov assumption is therefore understanding why all autoregressive models have a context window at all, and why extending that window is a fundamental research direction.
Exponential reduction: O(V^k) instead of O(V^T)
Requires large corpora to estimate higher-order n-grams reliably
O(k) memory for generation; no full history needed
Fixed context window; cannot condition on earlier tokens
Backoff and interpolation methods well understood
Complex smoothing schemes needed to avoid zero-probability n-grams
Well-defined stationary distribution; mixing time analysis
Real language is not Markovian — pronoun coreference spans many sentences
Interpretability is required (transition matrix is readable)
The task requires long-range dependencies (coreference, document summarisation)
Training data is limited (HMMs work with thousands of examples)
You have large data and compute budget (neural models dominate)
Inference speed is critical (no GPU needed)
The vocabulary is large and k > 3 (parameter explosion)
The task is genuinely memoryless (weather in some models)
The sequence has structural dependencies that violate the Markov property
You need a fast, interpretable baseline before neural models
Production accuracy requirements exceed what n-grams achieve
An n-gram model is a direct application of the kth-order Markov assumption to language modelling. The following is a complete, smoothed trigram model:

from collections import defaultdict, Counter
import math

class TrigramLM:
    """Trigram language model with Kneser-Ney-style backoff (add-k smoothing for simplicity)."""

    def __init__(self, k=0.1):
        self.k = k
        self.unigrams  = Counter()
        self.bigrams   = defaultdict(Counter)
        self.trigrams  = defaultdict(Counter)
        self.vocab     = set()
        self._N = 0

    def train(self, sentences):
        """sentences: list of lists of tokens (strings)"""
        for sent in sentences:
            padded = ["<s>", "<s>"] + list(sent) + ["</s>"]
            self._N += len(padded)
            for w in padded:
                self.unigrams[w] += 1
                self.vocab.add(w)
            for i in range(1, len(padded)):
                self.bigrams[padded[i-1]][padded[i]] += 1
            for i in range(2, len(padded)):
                self.trigrams[(padded[i-2], padded[i-1])][padded[i]] += 1
        return self

    def logprob(self, word, context):
        """Log P(word | context[-2:]) with add-k smoothing."""
        V = len(self.vocab)
        w1, w2 = context[-2], context[-1]

        # Trigram estimate
        tri_count   = self.trigrams[(w1, w2)][word]
        bi_context  = sum(self.trigrams[(w1, w2)].values())
        if bi_context > 0:
            prob = (tri_count + self.k) / (bi_context + self.k * V)
        else:
            # Backoff to bigram
            bi_count    = self.bigrams[w2][word]
            uni_context = sum(self.bigrams[w2].values())
            if uni_context > 0:
                prob = (bi_count + self.k) / (uni_context + self.k * V)
            else:
                # Backoff to unigram
                prob = (self.unigrams[word] + self.k) / (self._N + self.k * V)
        return math.log(prob)

    def sentence_logprob(self, sentence):
        tokens = ["<s>", "<s>"] + list(sentence) + ["</s>"]
        total = 0.0
        for i in range(2, len(tokens)):
            total += self.logprob(tokens[i], tokens[:i])
        return total

    def perplexity(self, sentences):
        total_log = 0.0
        total_words = 0
        for sent in sentences:
            total_log   += self.sentence_logprob(sent)
            total_words += len(sent) + 1   # +1 for </s>
        return math.exp(-total_log / total_words)

    def generate(self, max_len=20, seed=42):
        import random
        rng = random.Random(seed)
        tokens = ["<s>", "<s>"]
        for _ in range(max_len):
            ctx = (tokens[-2], tokens[-1])
            candidates = self.trigrams[ctx]
            if not candidates:
                break
            words, counts = zip(*candidates.items())
            total = sum(counts)
            r = rng.random() * total
            cumsum = 0
            chosen = words[-1]
            for w, c in zip(words, counts):
                cumsum += c
                if r < cumsum:
                    chosen = w
                    break
            if chosen == "</s>":
                break
            tokens.append(chosen)
        return tokens[2:]


# Usage
sentences = [
    "the cat sat on the mat".split(),
    "the cat chased the dog".split(),
    "the dog sat on a chair".split(),
    "a dog chased a cat through the park".split(),
]
lm = TrigramLM(k=0.01).train(sentences)
test = ["the cat sat".split()]
print(f"Perplexity on test: {lm.perplexity(test):.2f}")
print("Generated:", " ".join(lm.generate(max_len=10)))
Hidden Markov Model: A Practical Example

HMMs apply the Markov assumption to sequences with hidden (latent) states. The classic use case is part-of-speech tagging: the observed sequence is words, the hidden sequence is POS tags. The Viterbi algorithm finds the most probable hidden state sequence in O(T * N^2) time using the Markov assumption.

import numpy as np

class HMM:
    """First-order Hidden Markov Model."""

    def __init__(self, n_states, n_obs):
        self.N = n_states
        self.M = n_obs
        # A[i,j] = P(state j | state i)
        # B[i,k] = P(obs k | state i)
        # pi[i]  = P(initial state i)
        self.A  = np.ones((n_states, n_states)) / n_states
        self.B  = np.ones((n_states, n_obs))    / n_obs
        self.pi = np.ones(n_states)              / n_states

    def viterbi(self, obs_seq):
        """Most probable hidden state sequence for obs_seq (list of int)."""
        T = len(obs_seq)
        delta = np.zeros((T, self.N))   # best prob of ending in state i at t
        psi   = np.zeros((T, self.N), dtype=int)  # backpointers

        delta[0] = self.pi * self.B[:, obs_seq[0]]
        for t in range(1, T):
            for j in range(self.N):
                scores = delta[t-1] * self.A[:, j]
                psi[t, j]   = np.argmax(scores)
                delta[t, j] = scores[psi[t, j]] * self.B[j, obs_seq[t]]

        # Backtrack
        path = [int(np.argmax(delta[-1]))]
        for t in range(T-1, 0, -1):
            path.insert(0, psi[t, path[0]])
        return path, delta[-1][path[-1]]

# Example: simple weather HMM
# States: 0=Sunny, 1=Rainy
# Observations: 0=Walk, 1=Shop, 2=Clean

A  = np.array([[0.7, 0.3], [0.4, 0.6]])
B  = np.array([[0.5, 0.4, 0.1],   # Sunny -> walk 50%, shop 40%, clean 10%
               [0.1, 0.3, 0.6]])   # Rainy -> walk 10%, shop 30%, clean 60%
pi = np.array([0.6, 0.4])

hmm = HMM(n_states=2, n_obs=3)
hmm.A, hmm.B, hmm.pi = A, B, pi

obs = [0, 1, 2, 2, 1, 0]   # Walk, Shop, Clean, Clean, Shop, Walk
path, prob = hmm.viterbi(obs)
state_names = ["Sunny", "Rainy"]
print("Observations:", ["Walk","Shop","Clean"][o] for o in obs)
print("Viterbi path:", [state_names[s] for s in path])
print(f"Path probability: {prob:.6f}")
1. Setting k too high and ignoring sparsity

For a trigram model over a vocabulary of 50,000 words, there are 50,000^3 = 125 trillion possible trigrams. Even a large corpus covers only a tiny fraction. Using a naively high order without smoothing produces zero probabilities for most sequences.

# WRONG: no smoothing — almost all test n-grams are unseen
lm_no_smooth = TrigramLM(k=0)
lm_no_smooth.train(train_sentences)
# Many test sentences will have -inf log-probability

# RIGHT: use smoothing (add-k, Kneser-Ney, interpolation)
lm_smoothed = TrigramLM(k=0.01)
lm_smoothed.train(train_sentences)
print(f"Perplexity: {lm_smoothed.perplexity(test_sentences):.2f}")
2. Confusing Markov order with n-gram order
An n-gram language model is a (n-1)th-order Markov model. A trigram model (n=3) conditions on the previous 2 tokens — it is a 2nd-order Markov model. This off-by-one is a common source of confusion.

# N-gram order vs Markov order
# Unigram: P(w_t) — 0th-order Markov (no context)
# Bigram:  P(w_t | w_{t-1}) — 1st-order Markov
# Trigram: P(w_t | w_{t-2}, w_{t-1}) — 2nd-order Markov
# N-gram:  (N-1)th-order Markov

# So when someone says "use a bigram model" they mean first-order Markov
# and when they say "kth-order Markov" they mean a (k+1)-gram model
3. Using a fixed-order Markov model for sequences with long-range dependencies

For tasks like pronoun coreference ('The scientist... she') or document topic coherence, a fixed-order Markov model cannot access the relevant context. Using a trigram to answer 'what pronoun resolves to the entity mentioned 50 tokens ago' will always fail. Use a neural model or an explicit coreference component.
use Kneser-Ney smoothing for n-gram models in production. It is empirically the best-performing classical smoothing method and handles the sparsity of high-order n-grams.
perplexity is the standard metric for language model quality. Lower is better. Compare models at the same order k and smoothing method.
Choose k based on corpus size:
with 1M tokens, k=2 (trigram) is reliable. With 100M tokens, k=4 or k=5 becomes tractable. With less than 100k tokens, stick to k=1 (bigram).
For HMMs, use the Baum-Welch algorithm:
initialise with supervised labels if available, then refine with EM. Random initialisation often converges to poor local optima.
for time-series AR models, use the Akaike or Bayesian Information Criterion to select p rather than guessing.
Do not use discrete Markov chains for continuous-valued sequences:
discretise only if interpretability of the state space is required. Otherwise, use AR(p) or Kalman filter models.
N-gram (order k, vocab V, corpus T tokens)
HMM (N states, M obs, T steps)
The key scalability advantage of Markov models is that inference requires only the last k tokens —
memory regardless of sequence length. Transformers require
KV-cache memory per token in context, making long-context generation memory-intensive. This is why Markov models are still used in embedded systems and latency-critical applications.
n-gram language models, Laplace/Lidstone/Kneser-Ney smoothing. Use nltk.lm for out-of-the-box training.
Gaussian and multinomial HMMs with Baum-Welch (EM) training and Viterbi decoding. Standard library for HMM-based sequence labelling.
AR, ARMA, ARIMA models for time series. Includes AIC/BIC for order selection and diagnostic plots.
HMMs, Markov chains, and mixture models. Supports GPU acceleration.
extremely fast n-gram LM library used in production speech recognition and machine translation. Handles vocabularies of millions of words efficiently.

# NLTK trigram LM with Kneser-Ney smoothing
from nltk.lm import KneserNeyInterpolated
from nltk.lm.preprocessing import padded_everygram_pipeline

train_sents = [
    "the cat sat on the mat".split(),
    "the dog chased the cat".split(),
    "a cat sat on a chair".split(),
]

n = 3  # trigram = 2nd-order Markov
train_data, vocab = padded_everygram_pipeline(n, train_sents)

lm = KneserNeyInterpolated(n)
lm.fit(train_data, vocab)

# Generate text
from nltk.lm.preprocessing import pad_both_ends
import nltk
context = ("<s>", "<s>")
generated = [lm.generate(text_seed=context) for _ in range(10)]
print("Generated tokens:", generated)

# Perplexity
from nltk.lm.preprocessing import padded_everygram_pipeline
test_sents = ["the cat sat on a mat".split()]
test_data, _ = padded_everygram_pipeline(n, test_sents)
ppl = lm.perplexity([w for sent in test_sents for w in sent])
print(f"Perplexity: {ppl:.2f}")


# statsmodels AR(p) with automatic order selection
from statsmodels.tsa.ar_model import AutoReg, ar_select_order
import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
x = np.zeros(500)
phi = [0.6, 0.2]
for t in range(2, 500):
    x[t] = phi[0]*x[t-1] + phi[1]*x[t-2] + rng.normal(0, 0.5)

# Automatic order selection using BIC
sel = ar_select_order(x, maxlag=10, ic='bic')
print(f"BIC-selected order: {sel.ar_lags}")   # should select [1, 2]

model = AutoReg(x, lags=sel.ar_lags).fit()
print(model.summary())
In theory unbounded (vanishing gradient in practice)
O(d^2 * L) per layer * L layers
if training data can be modified by an adversary, injecting rare trigger n-grams that skew the transition matrix can cause targeted misclassification. Validate training data provenance.
computing P(w1, ..., wT) by multiplying T probabilities underflows to zero for T > ~100 even with float64. Always work in log-space and sum log-probabilities.
EM training can collapse a state's emission probability onto a single observation, producing a degenerate model that ignores most of the vocabulary. Add a small prior (Dirichlet smoothing) to B.

Markov models assume a stationary transition matrix. For time series with structural breaks (financial crises, pandemics), a fixed transition matrix will be poorly calibrated. Use regime-switching models or re-estimate frequently.
always fix random seeds in HMM training (EM initialisation) and n-gram generation. Log the exact training data hash and model parameters for audit.
states that the future is conditionally independent of the distant past given the recent k tokens: reduces the parameter space from exponential in T to polynomial in k.
a k-th order Markov model requires O(V^(k+1)) parameters instead of O(V^T) — the difference between tractable and intractable.
are the direct text-domain application: a trigram (n=3) is a 2nd-order Markov model. Always use smoothing (Kneser-Ney preferred) to handle unseen n-grams.
apply the Markov assumption over hidden states with probabilistic emissions. Viterbi decoding (O(T*N^2)) finds the most probable state sequence in polynomial time.
are the continuous-valued analogue: the current value is a linear function of the previous p values plus Gaussian noise. Parameters are estimated by OLS.
of an ergodic Markov chain is the left eigenvector of the transition matrix A with eigenvalue 1. Mixing time is governed by the second-largest eigenvalue.
for long-range dependencies: pronoun coreference, document topics, structural agreement across clauses. Neural models avoid this by representing the entire context window with continuous embeddings.
— the key practical advantage over Transformers (O(C * d) KV cache) for embedded or latency-critical applications.
multiplying T probabilities underflows to zero for T > 100. Sum log-probabilities instead.
